_schema: AI tools for trades — net-new category added 2026-05-28. Pricing is verified-as-of verified_date; many AI vendors are early-stage with volatile or sales-quoted pricing, flagged in pricing_summary. founded/headquartered are null/empty where not publicly verifiable. affiliate_url stays 'TBD' until tracking is confirmed.

ai_tools.name: Avoca AI
ai_tools.tagline: Enterprise AI front office for home-services trades — calls, follow-up, and CSR coaching
ai_tools.pricing_summary: Custom / sales-quoted only — no public pricing tiers. Priced by call volume and team size; contact Avoca for a quote
ai_tools.long_description:
Avoca is the best-funded AI company built specifically for the home-services trades. It raised more than $125 million across Seed, Series A, and Series B at a $1 billion valuation in April 2026 (Series B led by Meritech and General Catalyst, Series A by Kleiner Perkins), which makes it the clear category leader and largely answers the "will this AI startup still exist in two years?" question that hangs over the rest of the space.

Founded in 2023 by Tyson Chen and Apurva Shrivastava — the product was originally built for an HVAC company, Rescue Air — Avoca positions itself as an AI "front office" rather than a simple answering service. It has three pillars: Convert (AI answers inbound calls 24/7 and books jobs), Nurture (outbound follow-up with sub-15-second speed-to-lead on unbooked leads), and Coach (scores every CSR call 0-100 and auto-trains the team). It's a ServiceTitan marketplace partner and is aimed at larger HVAC, plumbing, electrical, and roofing operations.

The trade-off is access and fit: Avoca is enterprise-grade and sales-quoted only — there's no public pricing or self-serve signup — and it's overkill for a solo or small shop. But for a high-call-volume operation bleeding revenue through missed and mishandled calls, it's the most serious AI option in the trades today.
ai_tools.how_it_works: You connect Avoca to your phone system and CRM — it's a ServiceTitan marketplace partner, which is the most common enterprise FSM among larger trades shops. The Convert agent answers inbound calls 24/7 in natural voice, qualifies the caller, and books the job against your live schedule. Nurture runs instant outbound follow-up on unbooked leads (sub-15-second speed-to-lead), where even a few minutes of delay measurably cuts close rates. Coach records and scores every CSR call 0-100 against your criteria and surfaces coaching automatically. Because it's sold to larger operations, onboarding is hands-on: Avoca's team configures the agent to your services, pricing, and call flows, then tunes it on your real call data. Pricing is quoted based on your call volume and team size.

pros_detail.title: Funded category leader
pros_detail.explanation: $125M+ raised at a $1B valuation (April 2026) signals durability and heavy R&D investment, materially reducing the survival risk that shadows most year-old AI startups in this space.

pros_detail.title: Built specifically for the trades
pros_detail.explanation: Avoca was created for an HVAC company and is tuned on real trades call flows (HVAC, plumbing, electrical, roofing) — not a generic AI receptionist retrofitted onto contractors after the fact.

pros_detail.title: A full front office, not just answering
pros_detail.explanation: Convert + Nurture + Coach span inbound booking, outbound speed-to-lead, and CSR coaching — addressing the whole 'revenue from phone calls' problem rather than only picking up the phone.

pros_detail.title: Sub-15-second outbound follow-up
pros_detail.explanation: Nurture calls unbooked leads back almost instantly. Speed-to-lead is one of the most reliable levers on close rate, and few competitors automate outbound this aggressively.

pros_detail.title: CSR coaching from real calls
pros_detail.explanation: Scoring every call 0-100 and auto-generating coaching is a capability generic answering services simply don't offer — it improves your human team, not just the AI.

pros_detail.title: ServiceTitan integration
pros_detail.explanation: As a ServiceTitan marketplace partner, Avoca fits the enterprise FSM that most large trades operations already run, so it slots into existing workflows rather than replacing them.

cons_detail.title: Sales-quoted, no public pricing
cons_detail.explanation: There are no transparent tiers or self-serve signup. You go through a sales process to learn what it costs, which is slower and less buyer-friendly than the published pricing of SMB competitors.

cons_detail.title: Enterprise-priced
cons_detail.explanation: Avoca is built for high-call-volume operations, and the economics reflect that. It's not designed to be affordable for a solo operator or a small crew.

cons_detail.title: Overkill for small shops
cons_detail.explanation: The depth — outbound campaigns, CSR scoring and coaching — is far more than a one-to-five-person business needs. A small shop is better served by a simpler, cheaper AI receptionist.

cons_detail.title: Young company
cons_detail.explanation: Founded in 2023, Avoca has a short track record relative to established receptionist services, even with its heavy funding. Early adopters are betting on trajectory more than history.

cons_detail.title: Hands-on onboarding
cons_detail.explanation: This isn't a sign-up-and-go tool. Expect a configuration and tuning process with Avoca's team before the agent is performing the way you want.

ai_tools.best_for: Large home-services operations (HVAC, plumbing, electrical, roofing) that want an enterprise-grade AI front office to capture every inbound call, run instant lead follow-up, and coach their CSR team
ai_tools.weaknesses: Enterprise-priced and sales-quoted only (no public pricing or self-serve signup); overkill for solo or small shops; a young company despite heavy funding

ratings.reddit_sentiment: positive — funded category leader and ServiceTitan marketplace partner

ai_tools.affiliate_payout_note: No public affiliate program (enterprise sales-led) — we earn nothing from Avoca and include it purely on merit as the funded category leader

ai_faqs.a: Avoca is an AI 'front office' for home-services trades. It answers inbound calls 24/7 and books jobs (Convert), runs instant outbound follow-up on unbooked leads (Nurture), and scores and coaches your CSR team's calls (Coach). It's aimed at larger HVAC, plumbing, electrical, and roofing operations and is a ServiceTitan marketplace partner.

Avoca is sales-quoted only — there are no public pricing tiers or self-serve signup. Pricing is based on your call volume and team size, so you'll need to contact Avoca for a quote. It's positioned as an enterprise product, not a budget tool.

ai_faqs.q: Is Avoca worth it for a small shop?
ai_faqs.a: Generally no. Avoca's depth (outbound campaigns, CSR coaching) and enterprise pricing fit high-call-volume operations. A solo operator or small crew is usually better served by an affordable AI receptionist like Rosie or Goodcall.

Yes — Avoca is a ServiceTitan marketplace partner, which is a major reason it fits larger trades shops that already run ServiceTitan as their FSM. Confirm the current integration scope with Avoca during your evaluation.

ai_faqs.q: Is Avoca a stable company to build on?
ai_faqs.a: Avoca raised $125M+ at a $1B valuation in April 2026 from top investors, which significantly de-risks the 'will this startup survive?' concern common in early AI. It's still a young company (founded 2023), so weigh trajectory and funding against its short track record.

ai_tools.name: Rosie
ai_tools.pricing_summary: Professional $49/mo (answer calls, take messages, notify); Scale $149/mo (booking links, call transfers, in-call texting); Growth $299/mo (custom training files). 7-day free trial. US & Canada only
ai_tools.long_description:
Rosie is an AI answering service built specifically for home-service businesses — the HVAC tech, plumber, or electrician who's on a roof or under a sink all day and physically can't pick up a ringing phone. Starting at $49/mo, it's one of the most affordable trades-native AI receptionists, and it's designed around the exact way small contractors lose money: a missed call goes to voicemail, the caller hangs up, and they dial the next contractor on the list.

Rosie answers 24/7, and instead of making you script everything by hand, it scans your website and Google Business Profile to learn your services, pricing ranges, and service area, then uses that to answer caller questions accurately and book appointments straight into Google Calendar or Calendly. Bilingual English/Spanish support is included on every plan — meaningful in many trades markets. The tiers add capability rather than minutes: Professional ($49) answers and takes messages, Scale ($149) adds booking links, call transfers, and in-call texting, and Growth ($299) adds custom training files for more detailed responses.

The trade-offs are that it's a newer product with limited third-party review history, it's US/Canada only, and the genuinely useful features (booking, transfers) sit above the $49 entry tier. But for a solo or small home-service operator, it's a low-risk, low-cost way to stop leaking revenue to missed calls.
ai_tools.how_it_works: During setup, Rosie scans your website and Google Business Profile to learn what you do, your service area, and rough pricing, so it can answer callers without you scripting every response. You forward your calls (or your missed calls) to Rosie's number. When a customer calls, Rosie answers in natural voice, responds to questions about your services, and — on the Scale plan and up — checks your Google Calendar or Calendly availability, offers open times, and books the appointment automatically, then texts and emails you the details. It supports English and Spanish callers on every plan. There's a 7-day free trial so you can hear how it handles your real calls before committing.

pros_detail.title: Trades-native and genuinely affordable
pros_detail.explanation: Built for home-service businesses and starting at $49/mo, Rosie is one of the cheapest ways for a solo contractor to stop missing calls — far below enterprise platforms and most general AI receptionists.

pros_detail.title: Auto-learns from your website and Google profile
pros_detail.explanation: Rosie scans your site and Google Business Profile to learn your services, pricing, and area, so setup is fast and you don't have to hand-script every answer.

pros_detail.title: Books straight into your calendar
pros_detail.explanation: On Scale and up, Rosie checks Google Calendar or Calendly availability and books appointments automatically — turning a missed call into a booked job without you touching the phone.

pros_detail.title: Bilingual English/Spanish on every plan
pros_detail.explanation: Spanish-language support is included at all tiers, which matters in many trades service areas and is not universal among competitors.

pros_detail.title: 24/7 missed-call recovery
pros_detail.explanation: Rosie answers around the clock, so after-hours and overflow calls — a major source of lost revenue for field-based contractors — get captured instead of going to voicemail.

pros_detail.title: Low-risk 7-day trial
pros_detail.explanation: You can test Rosie against your real calls for a week before paying, which is the right way to evaluate any AI answering service.

cons_detail.title: Newer product, thin third-party reviews
cons_detail.explanation: Rosie doesn't yet have the deep G2/Capterra review history of established services, so there's less independent validation. Lean on the free trial to judge it on your own calls.

cons_detail.title: US and Canada only
cons_detail.explanation: Rosie is limited to the US and Canada, so it's not an option for contractors operating elsewhere.

cons_detail.title: Best features are above the entry tier
cons_detail.explanation: The $49 Professional plan answers and messages, but appointment booking, call transfers, and in-call texting require the $149 Scale plan — so the genuinely time-saving capability costs more than the headline price.

cons_detail.title: Not built for complex enterprise call flows
cons_detail.explanation: Rosie is designed for small home-service operators. High-volume operations needing outbound campaigns, CSR coaching, or deep CRM logic should look at an enterprise platform like Avoca instead.

cons_detail.title: Limited public company details
cons_detail.explanation: Founding and company background aren't prominently published, so buyers who weight vendor longevity heavily have less to go on. The product itself is what to evaluate.

ai_tools.best_for: Solo and small home-service contractors (HVAC, plumbing, electrical, handyman) who miss calls in the field and want an affordable 24/7 AI answering service that also books appointments
ai_tools.weaknesses: Newer product with limited third-party review history; US/Canada only; appointment booking and call transfers require the $149 tier rather than the $49 entry plan
ai_tools.affiliate_payout_note: No confirmed affiliate program — we currently earn nothing from Rosie

ai_faqs.a: Solo and small home-service contractors — HVAC, plumbing, electrical, handyman, and similar — who are in the field all day and miss inbound calls. At $49/mo to start, it's aimed at operators who want affordable, 24/7 call answering without an enterprise platform.

Three plans: Professional at $49/mo (answers calls, takes messages, sends notifications), Scale at $149/mo (adds booking links, call transfers, in-call texting), and Growth at $299/mo (adds custom training files). There's a 7-day free trial, and it's available in the US and Canada only.

Yes, on the Scale plan ($149/mo) and above. Rosie integrates with Google Calendar and Calendly, checks your availability, offers open times to the caller, and creates the appointment automatically — then sends you the details. The entry $49 plan answers and messages but doesn't book.
Yes — bilingual English/Spanish support is included on every plan, which is useful in many trades service areas where a meaningful share of callers prefer Spanish.

ai_faqs.q: Rosie vs a full AI front office like Avoca?
ai_faqs.a: Rosie is an affordable answering-and-booking service for small operators. Avoca is an enterprise front office (inbound + outbound + CSR coaching) for high-call-volume operations and is sales-quoted. Small shops almost always want Rosie; large operations that need outbound follow-up and coaching want Avoca.

ai_tools.name: Goodcall
ai_tools.tagline: Budget-friendly AI phone agent for service businesses
ai_tools.pricing_summary: Starter $79/mo ($66/mo billed annually); Growth $129/mo ($108 annual); Scale $249/mo ($208 annual). Unlimited minutes, no long-term contracts. 14-day free trial
ai_tools.long_description:
Goodcall is an AI phone agent aimed at small service businesses that want to stop missing calls without paying enterprise prices. Born out of the Google ecosystem — its founders came from Google — it lets you launch a custom AI voice agent in minutes with no engineering required, answering and automating inbound customer-service and sales calls.

Pricing is among the friendliest in the category: Starter at $79/mo (or $66 billed annually), Growth at $129/mo, and Scale at $249/mo, all with unlimited minutes and no long-term contracts, plus a 14-day free trial. That unlimited-minutes model is a real differentiator versus per-call or per-minute competitors if your call volume is unpredictable. The agent syncs with your CRM and calendar to find and book the best appointment slots and handle rescheduling, and it captures every inbound call, instantly sharing the details via SMS, email, Google Sheets, or your CRM. It's also HIPAA-compliant.

The main caveat is that Goodcall is general-purpose SMB software rather than trades-specific, so it lacks the trade-tuned call flows of Rosie or the front-office depth of Avoca, and it's best for businesses with relatively simple needs. But for a small contractor who wants an affordable, fast-to-launch AI receptionist with unlimited minutes, it's a strong value pick.
ai_tools.how_it_works: You build a custom AI voice agent in Goodcall's setup flow in a few minutes — no engineering team needed — defining what it should say, what it should collect, and how it should route or escalate. You connect your CRM and calendar so the agent can find open appointment slots, book, and reschedule. Then you forward your calls (or just missed/overflow calls) to Goodcall. The agent answers, handles customer-service and sales questions, and captures every call, instantly sharing the caller's details via SMS, email, Google Sheets, or your CRM so nothing falls through the cracks. All plans include unlimited minutes, and there's a 14-day free trial to test it on real calls.

pros_detail.title: Affordable with unlimited minutes
pros_detail.explanation: Starting at $79/mo (or $66 annually) with unlimited minutes and no contracts, Goodcall removes the anxiety of per-minute or per-call overages — valuable when your call volume is spiky or seasonal.

pros_detail.title: Launches in minutes, no engineering
pros_detail.explanation: You can stand up a working AI voice agent quickly through a guided setup, so a small shop without technical staff can get answering calls the same day.

pros_detail.title: CRM and calendar sync for booking
pros_detail.explanation: The agent connects to your calendar and CRM to find slots, book, and reschedule, turning inbound calls into booked appointments rather than just messages.

pros_detail.title: Captures and shares every call
pros_detail.explanation: Call details are pushed instantly via SMS, email, Google Sheets, or your CRM, so you have a record and a follow-up trail for every inbound call.

pros_detail.title: No contracts plus a 14-day trial
pros_detail.explanation: Month-to-month with a free trial means low commitment — you can test it against real calls and walk away if it doesn't pull its weight.

pros_detail.title: HIPAA-compliant call handling
pros_detail.explanation: For businesses with any compliance sensitivity, Goodcall offers HIPAA-compliant handling, which not every budget AI receptionist provides.

cons_detail.title: General-purpose, not trades-specific
cons_detail.explanation: Goodcall serves many small-business types, so it lacks the trade-tuned call flows of a home-services-native tool like Rosie. You'll do more configuration to make it sound like a trades shop.

cons_detail.title: Best for simple needs
cons_detail.explanation: It shines for straightforward answer-qualify-book workflows. Complex routing, outbound campaigns, and CSR coaching aren't its focus — operations needing that should look at Avoca.

cons_detail.title: Limited public company details
cons_detail.explanation: Beyond its Google-ecosystem origins, founding and company specifics aren't prominently published, so vendor-longevity-focused buyers have less to evaluate.

cons_detail.title: Depth scales with price
cons_detail.explanation: The more capable booking and integration features land on the Growth ($129) and Scale ($249) tiers, so the cheapest plan is more basic than the headline suggests.

cons_detail.title: Not home-service-specialized call handling
cons_detail.explanation: It won't natively understand trade jargon and workflows the way a purpose-built trades tool does; expect to invest setup time tuning its responses.

ai_tools.best_for: Very small service businesses that want an affordable, quick-to-launch AI phone agent with unlimited minutes and CRM/calendar booking
ai_tools.weaknesses: General-purpose rather than trades-specific; best for simple call-handling needs; less depth than enterprise platforms like Avoca

ratings.reddit_sentiment: generally positive for very small businesses with simple needs

ai_tools.affiliate_payout_note: No confirmed affiliate program — we currently earn nothing from Goodcall

ai_faqs.a: Three plans, all with unlimited minutes and no long-term contracts: Starter at $79/mo ($66 billed annually), Growth at $129/mo ($108 annual), and Scale at $249/mo ($208 annual). There's a 14-day free trial. The unlimited-minutes model is a differentiator versus per-call competitors.

ai_faqs.q: Is Goodcall good for contractors specifically?
ai_faqs.a: It works for contractors, but Goodcall is general small-business software rather than a trades-native tool, so you'll spend more setup time tuning its responses to your trade. If you want something pre-built for home services, Rosie is more trades-specific; Goodcall wins on unlimited minutes and value.

Yes — it syncs with your CRM and calendar to find open slots, book, and handle rescheduling, and it captures every call's details via SMS, email, Google Sheets, or your CRM. The deeper booking and integration capabilities are strongest on the Growth and Scale tiers.
No — you can launch a custom AI voice agent in a few minutes through its guided setup, with no engineering team required. That fast, no-code setup is one of its main selling points for small shops.

Goodcall is the budget generalist with unlimited minutes; Rosie is the trades-native, low-cost option built for home services; Avoca is the enterprise front office for high-volume operations. Very small shops choose Goodcall or Rosie on price and fit; larger operations choose Avoca for depth.

ai_tools.name: Smith.ai
ai_tools.tagline: Established AI + human hybrid receptionist with 24/7 call answering
ai_tools.pricing_summary: AI Receptionist from ~$97.50/mo with per-call pricing (~$9.75 per additional call); hybrid AI + live human receptionist from ~$292.50/mo. No setup fees, no contracts, 30-day money-back guarantee
ai_tools.long_description:
Smith.ai is one of the most established players in the receptionist space, and unlike the newer pure-AI startups it offers a hybrid model: an AI Receptionist for fully automated answering, or AI plus live human receptionists for the calls that genuinely need a person. Founded in 2015, it predates the current AI wave and brings a track record the venture-backed newcomers simply can't match yet.

The AI Receptionist starts around $97.50/mo with simple per-call pricing (roughly $9.75 per additional call) — no setup fees, no contracts, and a 30-day money-back guarantee — while the hybrid plans with live human receptionists start around $292.50/mo. Smith.ai answers 24/7, books appointments, qualifies leads, and routes or escalates calls, integrating with common CRMs and calendars (including via Zapier).

For a contractor who wants the reassurance of human backup on complex or high-value calls — or who simply trusts an established brand over a year-old startup — the hybrid model is a genuine differentiator. The trade-offs: Smith.ai is pricier than pure-AI options, per-call overages add up at high call volume, and it's general-purpose rather than trades-specific. But as a proven, lower-risk option with a human safety net, it's a strong pick for shops that value reliability over rock-bottom price.
ai_tools.how_it_works: You choose between the AI Receptionist (fully automated) or a hybrid plan that adds live human receptionists for calls the AI should hand off. You forward your calls (all of them, or just overflow and after-hours) to Smith.ai. The receptionist — AI or human — answers 24/7, follows your instructions to qualify leads, answer common questions, book appointments on your calendar, and route or escalate as needed. Billing is per client call rather than per minute, so a long call doesn't cost more than a short one; additional calls beyond your plan run about $9.75 each. Smith.ai integrates with common CRMs and calendars (including through Zapier), and there are no setup fees or contracts, with a 30-day money-back guarantee to de-risk trying it.

pros_detail.title: Established track record
pros_detail.explanation: Founded in 2015, Smith.ai has years of operating history answering business calls — a meaningful reassurance versus AI receptionists that are barely a year old.

pros_detail.title: Hybrid AI + human option
pros_detail.explanation: You can route complex or high-value calls to real human receptionists, not just AI. For contractors who worry about an AI fumbling a big lead, that human safety net is a genuine differentiator.

pros_detail.title: Transparent per-call pricing, no contracts
pros_detail.explanation: Billing is per client call with published rates, no setup fees, and no long-term contract — straightforward to predict and easy to leave.

pros_detail.title: 30-day money-back guarantee
pros_detail.explanation: A full month-back guarantee lowers the risk of trying it, effectively giving you a real-world trial period on your actual calls.

pros_detail.title: 24/7 answering, booking, and lead qualification
pros_detail.explanation: Round-the-clock coverage that books appointments and qualifies leads captures after-hours and overflow demand that would otherwise go to voicemail.

pros_detail.title: Broad CRM and calendar integrations
pros_detail.explanation: Smith.ai connects with common CRMs and calendars (including via Zapier), so booked calls and lead details flow into the tools you already use.

cons_detail.title: Pricier than pure-AI options
cons_detail.explanation: Starting around $97.50/mo for AI-only — and far more for human coverage — Smith.ai costs more than budget AI receptionists like Rosie or Goodcall. You're partly paying for the brand and the human option.

cons_detail.title: Per-call overages add up
cons_detail.explanation: At roughly $9.75 per additional call, a high-call-volume shop can see the bill climb quickly versus unlimited-minute competitors like Goodcall.

cons_detail.title: Human coverage is materially more expensive
cons_detail.explanation: The hybrid live-receptionist plans start around $292.50/mo — a big step up from the AI-only tier — so the most reassuring version of Smith.ai is also the priciest.

cons_detail.title: General-purpose, not trades-tuned
cons_detail.explanation: Smith.ai serves many industries, so it isn't pre-built around trades call flows the way Rosie is. Expect to give it clear instructions for your specific services.

cons_detail.title: AI-only tier is newer than the human service
cons_detail.explanation: Smith.ai's reputation was built on live human receptionists; its pure-AI offering is more recent, so judge the AI tier on a trial rather than assuming it matches the maturity of the human service.

ai_tools.best_for: Small businesses, including trades, that want a proven receptionist service with the option of human backup on complex calls — not pure AI alone
ai_tools.weaknesses: More expensive than pure-AI competitors, especially for live-human coverage; per-call overages add up at high call volume; general-purpose rather than trades-specific
ai_tools.affiliate_payout_note: No confirmed affiliate program — we currently earn nothing from Smith.ai

ai_faqs.q: What makes Smith.ai different from pure-AI receptionists?
ai_faqs.a: Smith.ai offers a hybrid model — you can use AI-only answering, or AI plus live human receptionists for calls that need a person. Most newer competitors are AI-only. That human backup, plus a track record dating to 2015, is the main reason contractors choose Smith.ai over a pure-AI startup.

The AI Receptionist starts around $97.50/mo with per-call pricing (about $9.75 per additional call). Hybrid plans with live human receptionists start around $292.50/mo. There are no setup fees or contracts, and a 30-day money-back guarantee. Per-call billing means call length doesn't change the cost.

Yes, though it's general-purpose rather than trades-specific. It works well for contractors who want reliability and the option of human receptionists on big calls. If you want a cheaper, trades-native AI option, compare it against Rosie; if you want the lowest cost with unlimited minutes, compare Goodcall.

ai_faqs.q: Does Smith.ai charge per minute or per call?
ai_faqs.a: Per client call, not per minute — so a long, productive call doesn't cost more than a short one. Additional calls beyond your plan run about $9.75 each, which is worth modeling against your typical monthly call volume.

There's no free trial in the usual sense, but Smith.ai offers a 30-day money-back guarantee with no setup fees or contracts, which effectively lets you test it on real calls for a month and get a refund if it isn't working for you.

ai_tools.name: Hover
ai_tools.tagline: AI measurement and 3D exterior models from phone photos
ai_tools.pricing_summary: Per-project measurement pricing (expedited delivery $19-$39/project) plus a Pro plan at $999/yr; interior scans $29-$89 by area. No simple flat monthly tier
ai_tools.long_description:
Hover turns a handful of smartphone photos into a precise, fully-dimensioned 3D model of a home's exterior — roof, siding, walls, windows, doors, and gutters. For roofing and exterior-remodeling contractors, that replaces ladders, tape measures, and the liability of climbing a roof to measure it, and it feeds straight into material ordering and estimates. Founded in 2011 in San Francisco and backed by Google Ventures, Menlo Ventures, and The Home Depot (over $87M raised), Hover is one of the most established AI-measurement tools in the trades.

The AI is the product: a rep or homeowner takes guided photos, and Hover's computer vision builds an accurate 3D model with measurements, which the contractor uses to order the right amount of material and produce a credible estimate. It covers roofing, siding, gutters, windows, doors, and full exterior projects, and integrates with roofing CRMs and material suppliers (it's deeply tied into the ABC Supply ecosystem).

The trade-off is the pricing model. Hover isn't a simple monthly subscription — it's per-project measurement pricing (expedited delivery runs $19-$39/project) plus a $999/year Pro plan for savings and premium features, with interior scans priced by area ($29-$89). For a low-volume contractor the per-project cost is manageable; for a high-volume shop the math needs checking against an aerial-report tool like EagleView. Reviewers consistently praise the measurement accuracy and 3D visualization.
ai_tools.how_it_works: A rep or the homeowner takes a series of guided photos of the property on a phone (Hover walks them through the angles). Hover's AI processes the images into a dimensionally-accurate 3D model of the exterior — roof planes, siding, walls, windows, doors, and gutters — typically within a short turnaround (expedited delivery is available per project). From the model, the contractor gets exact measurements to order materials and build an estimate, and can produce a 3D visualization to show the homeowner. Hover integrates with roofing CRMs and material suppliers so measurements and orders flow into the rest of the workflow. Pricing is per project for measurements plus an optional $999/yr Pro plan.

pros_detail.title: Accurate measurements without climbing
pros_detail.explanation: Hover produces dimensionally-accurate exterior measurements from phone photos, removing the safety risk and time of physically measuring a roof and reducing material-order errors.

pros_detail.title: Full exterior, not just roof
pros_detail.explanation: It models roof, siding, walls, windows, doors, and gutters — useful for exterior remodelers and full-home projects, not only roofing measurements.

pros_detail.title: 3D visualization helps close
pros_detail.explanation: The 3D model doubles as a sales tool — showing a homeowner a visualization of their home (and design options) helps close exterior projects, not just measure them.

pros_detail.title: Well-funded and established
pros_detail.explanation: Founded in 2011 with backing from Google Ventures, Menlo Ventures, and The Home Depot ($87M+ raised), Hover is a stable, mature tool rather than an unproven startup.

pros_detail.title: Material-ordering and CRM integrations
pros_detail.explanation: Tight integration with material suppliers (notably the ABC Supply ecosystem) and roofing CRMs means measurements flow into ordering and estimating rather than living in a silo.

cons_detail.title: Per-project pricing can add up
cons_detail.explanation: Hover isn't a simple flat subscription — measurements are priced per project (expedited $19-$39 each) plus a $999/yr Pro plan. High-volume shops should model the cost against aerial-report alternatives like EagleView.

cons_detail.title: Requires on-site photos
cons_detail.explanation: Because the model is built from ground-level phone photos, someone has to physically visit and photograph the property — unlike aerial-imagery tools (EagleView) that can measure remotely from existing imagery.

cons_detail.title: Photo quality affects results
cons_detail.explanation: Accuracy depends on following the guided-capture process well; rushed or incomplete photo sets can require recapture, adding a step versus a remote aerial report.

cons_detail.title: Exterior-focused
cons_detail.explanation: Hover is built for exterior measurement and visualization. It's not an FSM, estimating-CRM, or interior-trade tool — it's a measurement layer that feeds your other systems.

cons_detail.title: Pricing transparency is mixed
cons_detail.explanation: Between per-project fees, the Pro plan, and interior-scan pricing, the all-in cost depends heavily on your volume and mix — it takes some modeling to know what you'll actually pay.

ai_tools.best_for: Roofing and exterior-remodeling contractors that want accurate measurements and 3D visualizations from phone photos to speed up material ordering and estimates
ai_tools.weaknesses: Per-project pricing isn't a simple subscription and adds up at volume; requires someone on-site to photograph the property (unlike remote aerial tools); exterior-only

ratings.reddit_sentiment: positive — consistently praised for measurement accuracy and 3D visualization

ai_tools.affiliate_payout_note: No confirmed affiliate program — we currently earn nothing from Hover

ai_faqs.a: You take a set of guided phone photos of a property, and Hover's AI turns them into a dimensionally-accurate 3D model of the exterior (roof, siding, walls, windows, doors, gutters). Contractors use the measurements to order materials and build estimates, and the 3D model doubles as a sales visualization for the homeowner.

Hover uses per-project measurement pricing rather than a simple monthly subscription — expedited delivery runs $19-$39 per project — plus an optional Pro plan at $999/year for savings and premium features, and interior scans priced $29-$89 by area. Model your project volume to estimate real cost.

ai_faqs.q: Hover vs EagleView — which should I use?
ai_faqs.a: Hover builds its model from on-site phone photos and covers the full exterior (roof, siding, windows) plus 3D visualization; EagleView measures remotely from aerial imagery and is roof-measurement-focused with AI damage detection. Shops that want full-exterior + a sales visualization and don't mind photographing on-site lean Hover; shops that want fast remote roof reports lean EagleView. Many roofers use both.

ai_faqs.q: Is Hover accurate enough to order materials?
ai_faqs.a: Yes — accurate material ordering is its core use case, and reviewers consistently praise its measurement accuracy. Results depend on following the guided photo-capture process; incomplete photo sets can require recapture, so train reps on the capture flow.

ai_faqs.q: Does Hover replace my CRM or estimating software?
ai_faqs.a: No — Hover is a measurement-and-visualization layer, not an FSM or estimating CRM. It integrates with roofing CRMs and material suppliers so its measurements feed your existing estimating and ordering workflow rather than replacing it.

ai_tools.name: EagleView
ai_tools.tagline: Aerial roof measurement reports with AI damage detection
ai_tools.pricing_summary: Pay-as-you-go ~$15-$38 per standard report (up to $87 premium); EagleView One subscription is quote-based for contractors ordering 15+ reports/month
ai_tools.long_description:
EagleView is the most established name in aerial roof measurement — it pulls from its own large geospatial imagery library (covering ~94% of the US population) to deliver accurate roof measurement reports remotely, without anyone visiting or climbing the property. Founded in 2008 in Rochester, New York, it holds 300+ patents and is widely regarded as the most accepted aerial-measurement source in the roofing and insurance ecosystem, which matters when a measurement has to be trusted by an insurer on a claim.

The AI layer includes automated damage detection on top of the core measurement reports, and the data feeds estimating and insurance-restoration workflows. For storm-restoration and insurance-heavy roofers, EagleView's credibility with carriers is often the deciding factor over cheaper alternatives.

The trade-offs are price and coverage edges. Pricing is pay-as-you-go (roughly $15-$38 per standard report, up to $87 for premium), with the quote-based EagleView One subscription aimed at contractors ordering 15+ reports a month. Reviewers consistently call it the best and most-respected tool but openly debate whether the price premium is worth it versus competitors, and note it can struggle on very new homes without current satellite imagery or where heavy tree cover obstructs the roof. Its G2 rating sits around 3.8 (a small review base), reflecting that quality-vs-price tension.
ai_tools.how_it_works: Instead of measuring on-site, you order a report for an address and EagleView generates it from its existing aerial-imagery library — no site visit or roof climb required. The standard report returns accurate roof measurements (areas, pitches, lengths) used to build estimates and order materials, and premium reports add more detail; AI damage detection flags likely damage for restoration and insurance work. Reports are widely accepted by insurers, which streamlines claims. Pricing is pay-as-you-go per report (~$15-$38 standard), or via the quote-based EagleView One subscription for higher-volume contractors (15+ reports/month).

pros_detail.title: Remote measurement — no site visit
pros_detail.explanation: EagleView measures from its own aerial imagery, so you can get a roof report for an address without anyone driving out or climbing the roof — a major time and safety advantage over on-site tools.

pros_detail.title: Most-accepted by insurers
pros_detail.explanation: EagleView is the most widely accepted aerial-measurement source in the insurance ecosystem. For storm-restoration and claims work, that carrier credibility is often worth the premium on its own.

pros_detail.title: AI damage detection
pros_detail.explanation: On top of measurements, AI-powered damage detection helps restoration roofers identify and document likely damage, supporting estimates and insurance supplements.

pros_detail.title: Deep imagery library and patents
pros_detail.explanation: 300+ patents and imagery covering ~94% of the US population mean broad coverage and a defensible data moat — it's the category's incumbent for good reason.

pros_detail.title: Flexible pay-as-you-go pricing
pros_detail.explanation: You can order single reports (~$15-$38) without a subscription, then move to the quote-based EagleView One plan once you're ordering 15+/month — so low-volume shops aren't locked into a contract.

cons_detail.title: Price premium
cons_detail.explanation: EagleView is widely seen as the best product but also among the most expensive. Contractors openly debate whether the benefits outweigh the price differential versus cheaper measurement tools.

cons_detail.title: Struggles on new homes / no current imagery
cons_detail.explanation: Because it relies on existing aerial imagery, very new homes that aren't yet in the imagery library can't be measured, and you fall back to another method.

cons_detail.title: Tree obstructions complicate results
cons_detail.explanation: Heavy tree cover over a roof can obstruct the aerial view and complicate the measurement — a known limitation of imagery-based measurement.

cons_detail.title: Roof-measurement-focused
cons_detail.explanation: EagleView centers on roof measurement and property data; it's not a full-exterior visualization tool like Hover, nor an estimating CRM. It's a data source that feeds your other systems.

cons_detail.title: Thin third-party review base
cons_detail.explanation: Its G2 rating (~3.8 across a small number of reviews) is mixed and thin, driven largely by the price-vs-value debate. Judge it on the carrier-acceptance and accuracy your workflow actually needs.

ai_tools.best_for: Roofing and storm-restoration contractors — especially insurance-heavy shops — that want fast, remote, carrier-accepted roof measurement reports with AI damage detection
ai_tools.weaknesses: Price premium versus competitors; can't measure very new homes lacking current aerial imagery; tree cover obstructs results; roof-measurement-focused rather than full-exterior or estimating

ratings.reddit_sentiment: respected as the most-accepted aerial measurement tool, but pricing complaints are common

ai_tools.affiliate_payout_note: No confirmed affiliate program — we currently earn nothing from EagleView

ai_faqs.q: How much does an EagleView report cost?
ai_faqs.a: Pay-as-you-go pricing runs roughly $15-$38 per standard report, up to $87 for premium reports. Contractors ordering 15+ reports per month can move to the quote-based EagleView One subscription. There's no contract required for single pay-as-you-go reports.

ai_faqs.q: Why do roofers use EagleView over cheaper tools?
ai_faqs.a: Mostly carrier acceptance. EagleView is the most widely accepted aerial-measurement source among insurers, so for storm-restoration and claims work its reports are trusted with minimal pushback. That credibility — plus accuracy and AI damage detection — is why many roofers pay the premium despite cheaper alternatives existing.

EagleView measures remotely from aerial imagery (no site visit) and is roof-focused with strong insurer acceptance; Hover builds a full-exterior 3D model from on-site phone photos and doubles as a sales visualization. Insurance-heavy roofers often prefer EagleView; exterior remodelers wanting visualization lean Hover. Many shops use both depending on the job.

Because it relies on existing aerial imagery, it can't measure very new homes that aren't in its imagery library yet, and heavy tree cover can obstruct the roof view. It's also priced at a premium and is roof-measurement-focused rather than a full estimating or visualization platform.

Yes — it offers AI-powered damage detection on top of measurements, which helps restoration roofers identify and document likely damage to support estimates and insurance supplements. Combined with carrier-accepted measurements, that's a strong fit for claims-driven work.

ai_tools.name: Podium
ai_tools.tagline: AI lead conversion, messaging, and reviews for local service businesses
ai_tools.pricing_summary: Core $399/mo, Pro $599/mo, Signature custom. Includes its AI agent (Jerry) on AI plans. Widely noted as expensive for small businesses
ai_tools.long_description:
Podium is a lead-conversion and customer-communication platform built for local service businesses, and it has leaned hard into AI: its agent 'Jerry' (built on GPT-5.1) handles lead capture, scheduling, service requests, sales, and follow-up over text across channels, with tailored agents shipped for home services out of the box. Founded in 2014 in Lehi, Utah, Podium has grown into a major player ($219.9M revenue, 6,500+ customers) and holds a strong 4.6/5 G2 rating across 2,000+ reviews.

The core idea: most local-business leads come in by phone, web chat, or text and go cold fast. Podium consolidates those channels, uses AI to respond and convert leads instantly, automates review requests to build reputation, and folds in webchat, text marketing, and payments — all in one platform. For a home-service business losing leads to slow response, the AI texting-and-conversion layer is the pitch.

The trade-offs are price and overlap. At $399/mo (Core) to $599/mo (Pro) plus custom Signature pricing, Podium is expensive for small shops — a common complaint even among fans. And because it spans messaging, reviews, marketing, and payments, it overlaps with features some FSM platforms already include, so it's worth mapping against what your field-service software already does before adding it.
ai_tools.how_it_works: You connect Podium to your phone number, website, and review profiles, and it consolidates calls, texts, web chat, and social messages into one inbox. Its AI agent (Jerry) responds to inbound leads instantly over text — answering questions, qualifying, scheduling, and following up — so leads don't go cold while you're on a job. Podium automatically requests reviews after jobs to build your reputation, and adds text marketing campaigns and payment collection (text-to-pay) on top. Tailored AI agents for home services come preconfigured. Plans run Core $399/mo, Pro $599/mo, and a custom Signature tier.

pros_detail.title: AI lead conversion over text
pros_detail.explanation: Podium's AI agent responds to inbound leads instantly via text — qualifying, scheduling, and following up — which directly attacks the slow-response problem that loses local-service leads to competitors.

pros_detail.title: Consolidated customer communication
pros_detail.explanation: Calls, texts, web chat, and social messages land in one inbox, so a home-service business stops juggling channels and missing messages across phone, website, and social.

pros_detail.title: Automated review generation
pros_detail.explanation: Podium automates post-job review requests, which is one of the most reliable ways for local service businesses to build the Google reputation that drives inbound calls.

pros_detail.title: Strong, well-reviewed product
pros_detail.explanation: A 4.6/5 G2 rating across 2,000+ reviews is among the highest in this set, with users praising centralized messaging and review automation — a real signal of value for local businesses.

pros_detail.title: Home-services-tailored AI
pros_detail.explanation: Podium ships AI agents tuned for home services out of the box, so the conversational AI understands the context better than a generic chatbot bolted onto a website.

cons_detail.title: Expensive for small businesses
cons_detail.explanation: At $399-$599/mo plus custom Signature pricing, Podium is a real budget line. Even satisfied users frequently note it's pricey for smaller teams — model the ROI against your lead volume.

cons_detail.title: Overlaps with FSM features
cons_detail.explanation: Messaging, reviews, and payments overlap with what some FSM platforms already include. Map Podium against your existing field-service software so you're not paying twice for the same capabilities.

cons_detail.title: Not a field-service platform
cons_detail.explanation: Podium handles the front-of-funnel (lead conversion, communication, reputation) but doesn't schedule, dispatch, or manage jobs. It's an add-on to your FSM, not a replacement.

cons_detail.title: Value scales with lead volume
cons_detail.explanation: The AI-conversion ROI depends on having enough inbound leads to convert. A very low-volume shop may not generate enough activity to justify the monthly cost.

cons_detail.title: General local-business tool
cons_detail.explanation: Podium serves many local-business verticals (auto, aesthetics, retail), so while it has home-services agents, it isn't trades-only — some workflows are broader than a pure trades tool.

ai_tools.best_for: Home-service businesses with steady inbound lead volume that want AI to convert leads over text instantly and automate review generation in one platform
ai_tools.weaknesses: Expensive for small businesses ($399-$599/mo+); overlaps with messaging/review/payment features in some FSM platforms; front-of-funnel only (not scheduling/dispatch)

ratings.reddit_sentiment: strong — praised for centralized messaging and review automation; pricing seen as high

ai_tools.affiliate_payout_note: No confirmed affiliate program — we currently earn nothing from Podium

ai_faqs.q: What does Podium do for a home-service business?
ai_faqs.a: Podium consolidates your calls, texts, web chat, and social messages into one inbox, uses its AI agent to instantly respond to and convert inbound leads over text, automates post-job review requests, and adds text marketing and text-to-pay. The core value is converting leads that would otherwise go cold from slow response.

Published plans run Core at $399/mo and Pro at $599/mo, with a custom Signature tier. It's widely considered expensive for small businesses, so weigh the cost against your inbound lead volume and how much revenue faster lead response would recover.

ai_faqs.q: Do I still need FSM software if I use Podium?
ai_faqs.a: Yes — Podium handles the front of the funnel (lead conversion, messaging, reviews, payments) but doesn't schedule, dispatch, or manage jobs. It sits alongside your field-service platform. Check for feature overlap (messaging, reviews, payments) so you're not paying twice.

Jerry is Podium's AI agent (built on GPT-5.1) that handles lead capture, scheduling, service requests, sales, and follow-up over text across channels, with home-services-tuned versions out of the box. It's the piece that responds to and converts leads instantly without a human having to drop what they're doing.

ai_faqs.q: Is Podium worth it for a small shop?
ai_faqs.a: It depends on lead volume. The AI-conversion and review-automation value is real, but at $399+/mo it needs enough inbound leads to pay off. A very small or low-volume shop may find it expensive relative to the return; a busy shop losing leads to slow response is where it earns its cost.

ai_tools.name: Birdeye
ai_tools.tagline: AI reputation, reviews, and messaging for multi-location local brands
ai_tools.pricing_summary: Starts around $299/mo (Starter, Growth, Premium tiers); scales by locations and features and can be steep for small teams
ai_tools.long_description:
Birdeye is a reputation and customer-experience platform built for local and multi-location businesses, and it markets itself as the #1 AI review-management platform. Founded in 2013 and serving 150,000+ businesses (past $100M ARR), it centers on the thing that drives local-service inbound calls: online reviews and reputation. Its AI assists with review responses, and the platform spans review generation and monitoring, multi-channel messaging (SMS, email, web chat, social), surveys, and reporting dashboards.

For a home-service business — especially one with multiple locations or aggressive growth goals — Birdeye's pitch is owning your online reputation at scale: systematically collecting reviews, responding with AI assistance, and surfacing them across Google, your website, and social to win the local-search visibility that generates calls. It's consistently rated at or near the top for reputation management by the major review platforms.

The trade-offs mirror Podium's: it starts around $299/mo and scales by locations and features, which can feel steep for a single-location small shop, and it's a reputation/communication layer rather than field-service software. Its breadth (reviews, messaging, surveys, social) is powerful for a multi-location brand but can be more than a one-truck operator needs. Mapped to the right buyer — growth-stage or multi-location home services — it's a strong, well-regarded choice.
ai_tools.how_it_works: Birdeye connects to your Google Business Profile, other review sites, and communication channels. After a job, it automatically requests reviews from customers and routes them to the sites that matter, then helps you monitor and respond (with AI-assisted responses) from one dashboard. It also runs multi-channel messaging (SMS, email, web chat, social), customer surveys, and reporting on reputation and sentiment, and surfaces reviews on your website and social profiles to boost local-search visibility. Pricing starts around $299/mo across Starter, Growth, and Premium tiers and scales with the number of locations and features you enable.

pros_detail.title: Top-rated reputation management
pros_detail.explanation: Birdeye is consistently rated #1 for reputation management by the major review platforms and is used by 150,000+ businesses. For a business whose inbound calls hinge on Google reviews, that focus and track record matter.

pros_detail.title: AI-assisted review responses
pros_detail.explanation: AI helps draft and manage review responses at scale, so a busy or multi-location operation can keep up with reviews across sites without a dedicated person doing it manually.

pros_detail.title: Strong multi-location support
pros_detail.explanation: Birdeye is built for multi-location brands, with per-location review and reputation management plus rollup reporting — a real advantage for a home-service company with several branches.

pros_detail.title: Broad customer-experience suite
pros_detail.explanation: Beyond reviews, it includes messaging, surveys, web chat, and dashboards, so reputation, communication, and feedback live in one platform rather than several tools.

pros_detail.title: Drives local-search visibility
pros_detail.explanation: Systematic review generation plus surfacing reviews on your site and social directly supports the local-search visibility that produces inbound home-service calls.

cons_detail.title: Expensive for small/single-location shops
cons_detail.explanation: Starting around $299/mo and scaling by locations and features, Birdeye can feel expensive for a single-location small business — its value compounds with scale and multiple locations.

cons_detail.title: Reputation layer, not FSM
cons_detail.explanation: Birdeye manages reviews, reputation, and communication but doesn't schedule, dispatch, or manage jobs. It's an add-on to your field-service software, not a replacement.

cons_detail.title: Feature breadth can be overkill
cons_detail.explanation: The full suite (reviews, messaging, surveys, social, dashboards) is powerful for a multi-location brand but more than a one-truck operator needs — you may pay for capability you won't use.

cons_detail.title: Overlaps with messaging/review tools
cons_detail.explanation: Its messaging and review features overlap with platforms like Podium and with some FSM built-ins. Decide which tool owns reviews/messaging so you're not duplicating spend.

cons_detail.title: General local-business platform
cons_detail.explanation: Birdeye serves many local-business verticals, not just trades, so while it works well for home services it isn't a trades-specific tool with trade-tuned workflows.

ai_tools.best_for: Growth-stage or multi-location home-service businesses focused on building online reputation — systematic review generation, AI-assisted responses, and customer messaging at scale
ai_tools.weaknesses: Expensive for single-location small shops ($299/mo+, scales by location); reputation/communication layer rather than FSM; feature breadth can exceed what a small operator needs

ratings.reddit_sentiment: strong — widely rated #1 for reputation management; can feel expensive for small teams

ai_tools.affiliate_payout_note: No confirmed affiliate program — we currently earn nothing from Birdeye

ai_faqs.a: Birdeye is an AI reputation and customer-experience platform. It automatically requests reviews after jobs, helps you monitor and respond to them (with AI assistance) across sites, runs multi-channel messaging and surveys, and surfaces reviews to boost local-search visibility — all aimed at building the online reputation that drives inbound local-service calls.

Pricing starts around $299/mo across Starter, Growth, and Premium tiers and scales by the number of locations and features you enable. It's powerful for multi-location brands but can feel expensive for a single-location small shop, so weigh it against how much your business depends on reviews.

ai_faqs.q: Birdeye vs Podium — what's the difference?
ai_faqs.a: Both are AI-driven local-business platforms with overlap, but the emphasis differs: Birdeye leads with reputation and review management (especially for multi-location brands), while Podium leads with AI lead conversion and texting. Reputation-focused, multi-location operations lean Birdeye; lead-conversion-focused shops lean Podium. Avoid running both for the same job.

ai_faqs.q: Is Birdeye worth it for a small home-service business?
ai_faqs.a: Its value compounds with scale and multiple locations. A single-truck operator may find the $299/mo+ pricing and broad feature set more than they need, while a growth-stage or multi-location home-service company focused on dominating local reviews gets strong ROI. Match it to where your business is.

ai_faqs.q: Does Birdeye replace my field-service software?
ai_faqs.a: No — Birdeye manages reviews, reputation, and customer communication, but it doesn't schedule, dispatch, or manage jobs. It sits alongside your FSM. Check for overlap with your FSM's built-in messaging/review features so you don't pay twice for the same capability.
